topic: Type System - repr(C) for Stable Layout

turns.content: Hey, I'm starting my new job and trying to understand why the compiler is complaining about field ordering when I write a struct that needs to go into a C library. It seems like Rust automatically rearranges fields for alignment even when I don't use #[repr(C)]?

That's a common misunderstanding, but you're right to question it. Without the #[repr(C)] attribute on your struct definition, the compiler does optimize field order based on alignment rules and packing efficiency for performance. When you add #[repr(C)], that guarantee forces the layout to match C exactly, preventing any reordering or padding changes that might break FFI calls.
So if I don't use repr(C), my struct will still be compatible with C as long as I just match the field names and types? The compiler will just reorder them internally?

Not exactly. While you can write code that compiles without #[repr(C)] in many cases, the memory layout might differ from what C expects due to padding or reordering for optimization. The compiler may insert hidden fields or rearrange existing ones to satisfy alignment constraints, which means your struct won't have a guaranteed stable layout matching C's definition.
I see now. So the #[repr(C)] attribute is specifically needed when I want the Rust struct to match C's memory layout bit-for-bit for FFI?

Exactly. The #[repr(C)] attribute ensures that your Rust type has a guaranteed stable layout matching C, which is essential for safe interop between languages through FFI. Without it, the compiler might reorder fields or add padding for optimization purposes.
But what about enums? Can I use #[repr(C)] on enums to make them compatible with C too?

Yes, you can apply #[repr(C)] to enums as well. For example, an enum like my_enum with variants defined in Rust order will have a layout that matches C if you use #[repr(C)], though note that the variant ordering might still differ from how C defines it depending on your specific representation choice.
That makes sense. So for my new job, I should always add #[repr(C)] to any struct or enum that needs to cross into a C library?

Generally yes, but be aware that padding rules apply differently with repr(C) compared to default Rust layouts. You might need to adjust your field order manually if certain combinations cause unexpected padding under the #[repr(C)] attribute.
Got it. One last thing though, what happens if I have a struct with repr(C) but one of its fields is an owned Box or Rc? Does that break anything?

That's actually tricky. While #[repr(C)] guarantees the layout of the struct itself, it does not guarantee the memory address stability for heap-allocated types like Box or Rc inside the struct. For true FFI safety with those types, you would typically use references instead.

topic: Type System - repr(C) for Stable Layout

turns.content: Hey, I was looking at the C structs your teammate wrote and noticed they use #[repr(C)]. Since we always try to keep our data structures matching C layouts when interfacing with external systems, does that mean Rust will automatically reorder fields if it thinks a better order exists?

Not at all. That is the exact point of #[repr(C)]. When you apply this attribute to a struct, the compiler guarantees that the memory layout matches C exactly with no reordering or padding optimization changes. It prevents the optimizer from rearranging fields for cache efficiency, which ensures FFI safety but can make your Rust code slower than it could be if performance mattered more than compatibility.

Got it, so I should just treat it like a strict contract. But what about the padding rules? If I have an integer and then a pointer in my struct, will Rust add extra bytes to align things up automatically even with #[repr(C)]?

Yes, padding is still required by the language spec. The compiler must ensure every field has proper alignment relative to its type size, so that pointer arithmetic and memory access work correctly on all platforms. That means #[repr(C)] does not disable padding rules; it only locks down the order of existing fields while you still get the necessary alignment bytes inserted where needed for safety.
That makes sense, but what about enums? If I define an enum with #[repr(C)] and have variants of different sizes, does Rust pick a specific variant as the zero value like C does?

Exactly. With #[repr(C)] on enums, Rust selects one specific variant to represent the zero value in memory, usually the first one defined unless you specify otherwise with #[repr(u8)]. This is critical for C interoperability because C expects a consistent zero representation across all codebases, so if your enum layout shifts without this rule, FFI calls could misinterpret data.
So the only time I should worry about variant order is when matching C enums? And if I don't use #[repr(C)] on an enum, can I still rely on it being zero-safe for FFI?

No. Without #[repr(C)], the compiler may reorder variants or choose a different zero variant based on internal heuristics, which breaks C expectations and causes undefined behavior when calling into C libraries. You must pin the representation explicitly to ensure that your Rust enum behaves identically to its C counterpart in memory.

topic: Type System - repr(C) for Stable Layout

turns.content: Why is #[repr(C)] actually necessary if the compiler can reorder fields for performance, unlike Java where field order matters more?

In Rust, the default behavior allows the compiler to rearrange struct fields based on alignment and size optimizations, which breaks C ABI compatibility. When you use #[repr(C)] on a struct, you force the compiler to preserve the exact declaration order without reordering or padding adjustments unless explicitly specified. This is critical for FFI because C compilers will generate code expecting that specific memory layout; if Rust reorders fields, your pointers won't match and the data will be corrupted. Java doesn't have this issue since it uses reference types and doesn't expose raw memory layouts to native code in the same way.
But what happens if I don't use #[repr(C)] on an enum? Can Rust optimize away padding that Java would require?

Without #[repr(C)], Rust may reorder variant data or apply aggressive optimizations that differ from C's strict layout rules. For example, a simple integer enum might get packed differently than expected by a C compiler because Rust doesn't guarantee zero-size variants in the same way. With #[repr(C)] on enums, each variant gets exactly the size you declare, preventing unexpected reordering. This matters when passing structures across FFI boundaries where mismatched sizes cause runtime errors.
Okay so padding is only guaranteed with #[repr(C)]? What about #[repr(align(N))] and how does that interact?

#[repr(align(N))] guarantees all fields will be aligned to N bytes, but it doesn't control field order or padding between fields. You can combine them like #[repr(C, align(8))] to get both C-compatible ordering and 8-byte alignment. Without #[repr(C)], even if you specify alignment, Rust might still reorder fields internally for efficiency. The key insight is that #[repr(C)] prevents any reordering while #[repr(align(N))] only affects individual field placement relative to the start of the struct.
So if I have a struct with i32 and f64 fields, will Rust automatically pad it differently than C expects without #[repr(C)]?

Yes. Without #[repr(C)], Rust might place the larger field first or optimize away padding that C requires for alignment. For instance, if you declare a struct with an i32 followed by f64, Rust could swap their order based on size and alignment rules. In contrast, #[repr(C)] ensures the f64 comes after the i32 exactly as written in your source code. This prevents memory corruption when interfacing with C libraries that expect the specific sequence of bytes.
This is frustrating because my side project compiled fine but then crashed when calling a C function. It seems like Rust was optimizing away something I thought would be safe.

That's exactly what happens when you forget #[repr(C)] on structs used in FFI. The compiler optimizes field order and padding based on internal heuristics, which differs from C's strict rules. When your Rust struct gets passed to a C function expecting the exact layout you declared, memory access patterns break because the pointers point to different offsets than anticipated. You can debug this by printing the actual size of the struct using std::mem::size_of() before and after adding #[repr(C)].
So I should always add #[repr(C)] whenever I'm dealing with FFI? What about when I just want to share data between Rust components?

#[repr(C)] is only strictly required for FFI cases where you need guaranteed memory layout matching C. For internal Rust code, the default field ordering and padding optimizations are fine since Rust's type system handles it correctly without needing to match another language's ABI. However, if you ever plan to expose structs to other languages or use them in shared libraries, #[repr(C)] becomes mandatory to avoid subtle bugs from unexpected reordering.
One more thing - what happens with union-like scenarios? Can I simulate unions in Rust and will they work the same way as C?

Rust doesn't have built-in unions but you can simulate them using enums with #[repr(C)] where each variant has zero size. For example, an enum with variants like SomeValue(i32) and OtherValue(f64) will only take the space of whichever variant is active at runtime. This mirrors C's union behavior where all variants share the same memory region. However, unlike C unions which allow multiple values to coexist in a single instance, Rust enums enforce a single active state per value.
That makes sense. So my final question: if I'm writing a library that will be used by both Rust and C projects, should I document the repr attributes explicitly?

Absolutely. Since #[repr(C)] affects memory layout in ways that aren't obvious from type names alone, your documentation must clearly state which types use #[repr(C)] and why. This prevents accidental mismatches when C developers assume default Rust behavior. You can also provide example structs with their repr attributes in the docs so users see exactly how the compiler handles each case before they start writing code.
